mission.title: Install and Configure Open Policy Agent Opa- on Kubernetes
mission.description: Open Policy Agent (OPA) is an open-source, general-purpose policy engine that enables unified, context-aware policy enforcement across your stack. Installing OPA allows you to manage and enforce policies consistently in your Kubernetes environment.

steps.title: Add the OPA Helm repository
steps.description:
First, add the official OPA Helm repository to your local Helm setup with the following command:
```bash
helm repo add open-policy-agent https://open-policy-agent.github.io/opa-helm
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install OPA using Helm
steps.description:
Now, install OPA in your Kubernetes cluster using Helm. Replace `<your-namespace>` with your desired namespace:
```bash
helm install opa open-policy-agent/opa --namespace <your-namespace> --create-namespace --version v1.14.0
```

steps.title: Verify OPA installation
steps.description:
Check if the OPA pod is running successfully:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace <your-namespace>
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits for OPA
steps.description:
To ensure OPA has appropriate resource limits, edit the deployment:
```bash
kubectl edit deployment opa --namespace <your-namespace>
``` 
Add the following resource limits under the `spec.template.spec.containers.resources` section:
```yaml
resources:
  limits:
    memory: "512Mi"
    cpu: "500m"
  requests:
    memory: "256Mi"
    cpu: "250m"
```

steps.title: Set up RBAC for OPA
steps.description:
Create a Role and RoleBinding to allow OPA to access necessary resources. Save the following YAML to a file named `opa-rbac.yaml`:
```yaml
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  namespace: <your-namespace>
  name: opa-role
rules:
- apiGroups: ["*"]
  resources: ["pods", "services"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: opa-role-binding
  namespace: <your-namespace>
subjects:
- kind: ServiceAccount
  name: opa
  namespace: <your-namespace>
roleRef:
  kind: Role
  name: opa-role
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
```
Then apply it:
```bash
kubectl apply -f opa-rbac.yaml
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of OPA will show the OPA pod running in your specified namespace. You can verify the configuration by checking the resource limits and RBAC settings applied to the OPA deployment.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add open-policy-agent https://open-policy-agent.github.io/opa-helm
helm repo update

resources:
  limits:
    memory: "512Mi"
    cpu: "500m"
  requests:
    memory: "256Mi"
    cpu: "250m"

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
To cleanly remove the OPA release from your Kubernetes cluster, use the following command, replacing `<your-namespace>` with your actual namespace:
```bash
helm uninstall opa --namespace <your-namespace>
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
If there are any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) or persistent resources associated with OPA, remove them using the following commands:
```bash
kubectl delete crd <crd-name>
# Replace <crd-name> with the actual CRD name if applicable
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
To remove the namespace created for OPA and verify that all resources are deleted, run the following commands:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace <your-namespace>
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading OPA, it's a good practice to back up the current configuration and state. You can export the current Helm release with:
```bash
helm get all opa --namespace <your-namespace> > opa-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and upgrade OPA to the desired version. Use the following commands:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade opa open-policy-agent/opa --namespace <your-namespace> --version v1.15.0
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
After upgrading, verify that the OPA pod is running the new version successfully:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace <your-namespace>
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If the OPA pod is in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for errors:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace <your-namespace>
# Replace <pod-name> with the actual pod name
``` 
Look for any configuration errors or resource limit issues.

troubleshooting.title: RBAC permissions issue
troubleshooting.description:
If OPA cannot access required resources, verify the Role and RoleBinding:
```bash
kubectl get role opa-role --namespace <your-namespace> -o yaml
kubectl get rolebinding opa-role-binding --namespace <your-namespace> -o yaml
``` 
Ensure that the permissions are correctly set.

troubleshooting.title: Helm release not found
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter an error stating that the Helm release is not found, ensure that you are using the correct namespace:
```bash
helm list --namespace <your-namespace>
``` 
This command will show all releases in the specified namespace.

troubleshooting.title: Resource limits causing OPA to be evicted
troubleshooting.description:
If OPA is being evicted due to resource limits, check the events for the pod:
```bash
kubectl describe pod <pod-name> --namespace <your-namespace>
``` 
Look for eviction messages and consider adjusting the resource limits.

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running and have installed Helm and kubectl on your local machine.
